title: Introduction
text:
The real world context of my topic is related to “Ocean Acidification” and the impact it has in our lives. This is a pressing issue in the world we live in today. My project is based on ocean acidification “Why do ocean acidification levels differ among the eight major marine and Great Lakes ecosystem in the US with different coastline”. My research question will compare the data of a few regions in the United States using the “NOAA, National Marine Ecosystem Status” site to the look into the casues of different acidification levels. This topic is relevant and it is intertwined with the MCPS curriculum unit “Acids and Bases” where students learn the cause and impacts of our changing ocean chemistry.
Our ocean absorbs a large amount of carbon dioxide to maintain a chemical equilibrium with the atmosphere. Because human activities have drastically raised atmospheric CO2 levels, the ocean takes up roughly 25% to 30% of these emissions to balance the difference, acting as a critical carbon sink. A carbon sink is any natural or artificial reservoir that absorbs and stores more carbon dioxide (CO₂) from the atmosphere than it releases. This absorption of carbon dioxide is increasing the acidity of our ocean, which has impacted marine life and people who depend on them. NOAA Pacific Marine Environmental Laboratory Carbon Group,has through their data shown ocean CO2 rising with an increase in atmospheric CO2. In turn, the pH of the ocean decreases, which reflects an increasd in acidity.
https://oceanacidification.noaa.gov/what-is-ocean-acidification/?utm_source=chatgpt.com
https://ecowatch.noaa.gov/national-status https://ecowatch.noaa.gov/thematic/ocean-acidification
https://ecowatch.noaa.gov/national-status

2 Official Research Questions
Your project must be guided by clear, measurable data science inquiries. Use the format below to define your scope.

Primary Research Question (RQ1): Is increasing ocean acidification causing a change in pH leading to an affect on marine organisms?
Secondary Research Question (RQ2): Evidence of indicators that data collected is usefulfor our marine ecosystem -coastal, and aquatic ecosystems.

2.1 Prior Work 1: Data Source:
Global observations of pCO2 from automated shipboard systems that take in and analyze water while a vessel is moving are aggregated in the Surface Ocean CO2 Atlas (SOCAT; Bakker et al., 2016). These shipboard observations have been combined with data from available observational, model-based, and satellite products (e.g., sea-surface temperature, salinity, and wind speed) to create estimates of surface ocean pCO2 in U.S. Large Marine Ecosystems over multiple decades, using a technique known as machine learning (Sharp et al., 2024). The pCO2 data shown here as an ecosystem indicator of ocean acidification represent regional annual averages of these mapped estimates. Uncertainty in pCO2 is estimated from a data-based validation of the machine-learning algorithms for each Large Marine Ecosystem, scaled by the data coverage in a given month.

title: Introduction
text:
3 Data Source Orientation
Global observations of pCO2 from automated shipboard systems that take in and analyze water while a vessel is moving are aggregated in the Surface Ocean CO2 Atlas (SOCAT; Bakker et al., 2016). These shipboard observations have been combined with data from available observational, model-based, and satellite products (e.g., sea-surface temperature, salinity, and wind speed) to create estimates of surface ocean pCO2 in U.S. Large Marine Ecosystems over multiple decades, using a technique known as machine learning (Sharp et al., 2024).
Description of Sea Surface pH:
Man-made (or “anthropogenic”) CO2 that is taken up by the ocean influences the chemistry of seawater, resulting in an increase in hydrogen ion concentration of the surface ocean (i.e. a decrease in surface ocean pH). pH can be viewed as a “master variable” in ocean chemistry, controlling acid-base balance in the sea. It is also a common way to quantify ocean acidification: average surface ocean pH has decreased by more than 0.1 units since the beginning of the industrial revolution (Jiang et al., 2023).
Data Source:
Global observations of pCO2 from automated shipboard systems that take in and analyze water while a vessel is moving are aggregated in the Surface Ocean CO2 Atlas (SOCAT; Bakker et al., 2016). These shipboard observations have been combined with data from available observational, model-based, and satellite products (e.g., sea-surface temperature, salinity, and wind speed) to create estimates of surface ocean pCO2 in U.S. Large Marine Ecosystems over multiple decades, using a technique known as machine learning (Sharp et al., 2024). The pCO2 data shown here as an ecosystem indicator of ocean acidification represent regional annual averages of these mapped estimates. Uncertainty in pCO2 is estimated from a data-based validation of the machine-learning algorithms for each Large Marine Ecosystem, scaled by the data coverage in a given month.
